title: Older adult fall prevention
content: Falls are common among adults age 65 and older, but many falls can be prevented. Helpful steps include removing home hazards, improving lighting, checking vision, reviewing medicines with a clinician or pharmacist, and doing strength and balance activities when appropriate. After a fall, urgent medical care is important if there is head injury, confusion, inability to stand, suspected fracture, severe pain, heavy bleeding, or anticoagulant use.

title: Emergency warning signs for adults
source: MedlinePlus When to use the emergency room - adult
content: Use emergency care for symptoms that may threaten life or cause permanent harm. Warning signs include severe chest pain or pressure, severe trouble breathing, fainting, sudden one-sided weakness or facial droop, sudden trouble speaking, seeing, walking, or understanding, head injury followed by fainting or confusion, seizure that lasts longer than about one minute or is followed by slow recovery, heavy bleeding, severe allergic reaction with breathing problems, and suspected fracture with inability to move.

title: Medicines and fall risk in older adults
content: Some medicines can increase sleepiness, dizziness, slower reaction time, orthostatic hypotension, or confusion, which may raise fall risk. Medicines to discuss with a clinician or pharmacist include sleep medicines, sedatives, anti-anxiety medicines, some pain medicines, some blood pressure medicines, antihistamines, and complex medication lists. Do not stop prescribed medicines without professional guidance.

title: Dehydration concerns in older adults
content: Older adults may need prompt medical evaluation when vomiting or diarrhea continues, fever is present, urine is much lower than usual, the mouth is very dry, dizziness occurs, weakness is severe, or mental status worsens. If symptoms are severe, the person cannot keep fluids down, or there is confusion or fainting, urgent care or emergency care may be needed.

title: Medical safety boundary
content: For elderly medical questions, provide health education, risk reminders, and guidance about when to contact a clinician or emergency services. Do not provide a definitive diagnosis, prescribe medicine, change medication doses, or replace a clinician. If the user describes emergency warning signs, the response should prioritize contacting local emergency services immediately. In Taiwan, call 119.
